title: Basel, Rotterdam, and Stockholm (BRS) Conventions
focus: Recent COP outcomes, specific chemical additions, and core mandates of the BRS conventions.
rationale: The BRS conventions are frequently tested together due to their overlapping focus on hazardous chemicals and wastes. The recent COP additions (specific chemicals) and core mechanisms (PIC, POPs) are prime UPSC targets.
sectionExcerpt: The 17th Basel, 12th Rotterdam, and 12th Stockholm Convention meetings in Geneva yielded key updates. Basel (hazardous wastes) launched work on textile waste and updated POPs guidelines. Rotterdam (PIC for hazardous chemicals) added Carbosulfan and Fenthion to Annex III. Stockholm (POPs) added Chlorpyrifos, LC-PFCAs, and MCCPs to Annex A, and amended UV-328 exemptions. India is a party to all three. Basel enforces Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for transboundary waste but excludes radioactive waste. Rotterdam promotes informed decisions on international trade of banned/restricted chemicals without outright banning them. Stockholm aims to protect health and environment from persistent organic pollutants.
evidenceBullets: Basel Convention (1989) enforces Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for transboundary hazardous wastes, excludes radioactive waste, and India is a party.
Rotterdam Convention (2004) creates a legally binding PIC procedure for international trade of certain hazardous chemicals/pesticides but does not outright ban them.
Stockholm Convention (2004) targets Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs); India ratified it in 2006.
Rotterdam COP-12 added Carbosulfan and Fenthion to Annex III.
Stockholm COP-12 added Chlorpyrifos, LC-PFCAs, and MCCPs to Annex A, and amended exemptions for UV-328 (a phenolic benzotriazole used as a UV stabilizer).

title: Minamata Convention and Mercury Pollution
focus: COP-6 outcomes, Minamata Convention provisions, and characteristics/toxicity of Mercury.
rationale: Mercury pollution is a classic environmental chemistry topic. The COP-6 targets (dental amalgams, skin-lightening) and the bioaccumulation of methylmercury are highly testable.
sectionExcerpt: COP-6 to the Minamata Convention on Mercury agreed to phase out mercury-based dental amalgams by 2034, eliminate mercury-added skin-lightening products, and strengthen action on artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM). The Minamata Convention is a legally binding treaty (effective 2017) with UNEP secretariat support; India is a member. Mercury is a naturally occurring heavy metal, liquid at room temperature, sourced from coal ash, thermal plants, and gold mining. Emitted mercury converts into highly toxic methylmercury, which bioaccumulates in aquatic food chains. There is no known safe level of mercury exposure for humans.
evidenceBullets: The Minamata Convention (effective 2017) is a legally binding treaty to protect human health and the environment from mercury; India is a member.
COP-6 agreed to phase out mercury-based dental amalgams by 2034 and eliminate mercury-added skin-lightening products.
Mercury is the only common metal that is liquid at room temperature; anthropogenic sources include coal-based thermal power plants and gold mining.
Mercury converts into highly toxic methylmercury, leading to bioaccumulation in fish and animals.
There is no known safe level of mercury exposure for humans.

title: Global Plastic Pollution Treaty
focus: INC negotiations, country groupings (Like Minded vs. High Ambition), and the historical evolution of plastic pollution treaties.
rationale: The ongoing negotiations for a legally binding plastic treaty are highly relevant. The divide between production-restricting countries and those opposing it (including India) is a key geopolitical and environmental issue.
sectionExcerpt: Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee (INC) talks for a Global Plastic Pollution Treaty, mandated by a 2022 UNEA resolution, recently adjourned. The treaty aims for an international legally binding instrument addressing the full plastic lifecycle. Disagreements persist: Like Minded Countries (including India, China, Saudi Arabia) reject restrictions on plastic production, whereas the High Ambition Coalition (around 100 countries, including the EU) argues production caps are essential. The evolution of global plastic regulation includes MARPOL Annex V (1988) banning ship-based plastic discharges, the 2019 Basel Convention amendments bringing plastic waste under PIC rules, and SDG 12 & 14.1.
evidenceBullets: The Global Plastic Pollution Treaty is being negotiated by the INC under a 2022 UNEA resolution to create a legally binding instrument addressing the plastic lifecycle.
Like Minded Countries, including India, China, and Saudi Arabia, reject restrictions on plastic production.
The High Ambition Coalition, including the European Union, advocates for essential production caps on plastics.
MARPOL Annex V (1988) was the first treaty banning plastics in ship-based discharges.
The 2019 Basel Convention Amendments brought plastic waste under global notification and Prior Informed Consent (PIC) rules.
